label: delphi-r1-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the leading cases and how courts have actually applied the rule to messy facts.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not, a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the surcharge non-compliant with art. XIII D, §6(b) — most plausibly the portion of the $0.85/hcf figure attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" beyond the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential, and/or the revenue-use structure (rebates flowing mostly to non-payers, conservation-education staffing) that looks like cross-subsidization rather than cost-of-service recovery. Full invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than partial invalidation/remand for recalculation, but the proposition as framed (\"in whole or in substantial part\") is likely satisfied either way. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is weak and unlikely to avoid the merits.","rationales":["Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 holds tiered water rates satisfy §6(b) only to the extent tied to actual cost of service at that tier and rejects freestanding conservation-goal pricing, and the district's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation tracks the precise defect that doomed San Juan Capistrano's rate structure.","Article XIII D, §6(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency in any legal challenge, and the district's concession that the rebate program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' will make it difficult to prove the fee is not cross-subsidizing other customer classes in violation of §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement.","The 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) is a genuine cost-of-service anchor that meaningfully distinguishes this case from Capistrano's total absence of a supporting cost study, making complete invalidation less likely than a court simply capping or remanding the rate to the cost-justified differential.","Directing 15% of proceeds to conservation-education staffing and 55% to rebates that primarily benefit non-payers implicates §6(b)(1)-(2)'s limits on using fee revenue for purposes broader than the cost of serving the paying parcels, independent of whether any portion of the rate differential itself is cost-justified.","The district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a 'property-related fee' is very likely to fail under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, which treats charges for metered/ongoing water service as property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, so the court will reach and likely rule against the district on the cost-of-service merits rather than dismiss on a threshold ground."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify holding re: cost-based tiered charges)","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) — verify citation and holding re: tiered/drought-surcharge cost justification","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (procedural/validation-action implications, not merits)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e) (Prop 26 tax definition and fee exceptions)","Cal. Const. art. X, §2 (conservation mandate)","Water Code §375 (conservation program financing authority)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"What dollar portion of the $0.85/hcf surcharge is actually attributable to the 1.6x cost differential versus pure conservation-incentive markup is not specified in the hypothetical, and this factual gap is likely dispositive of whether a court finds full compliance, partial invalidation, or total invalidation — without expert/rate-study evidence on this allocation, any probability estimate is necessarily a judgment call about how courts tend to resolve ambiguous cost-allocation records against the agency bearing the burden of proof."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not, a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the surcharge non-compliant with art. XIII D, §6(b) — most plausibly the portion of the $0.85/hcf figure attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" beyond the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential, and/or the revenue-use structure (rebates flowing mostly to non-payers, conservation-education staffing) that looks like cross-subsidization rather than cost-of-service recovery. Full invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than partial invalidation/remand for recalculation, but the proposition as framed ("in whole or in substantial part") is likely satisfied either way. The district's fallback "not a property-related fee" argument is weak and unlikely to avoid the merits.
rationales: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 holds tiered water rates satisfy §6(b) only to the extent tied to actual cost of service at that tier and rejects freestanding conservation-goal pricing, and the district's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation tracks the precise defect that doomed San Juan Capistrano's rate structure.

Article XIII D, §6(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency in any legal challenge, and the district's concession that the rebate program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' will make it difficult to prove the fee is not cross-subsidizing other customer classes in violation of §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement.

The 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) is a genuine cost-of-service anchor that meaningfully distinguishes this case from Capistrano's total absence of a supporting cost study, making complete invalidation less likely than a court simply capping or remanding the rate to the cost-justified differential.

Directing 15% of proceeds to conservation-education staffing and 55% to rebates that primarily benefit non-payers implicates §6(b)(1)-(2)'s limits on using fee revenue for purposes broader than the cost of serving the paying parcels, independent of whether any portion of the rate differential itself is cost-justified.

The district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a 'property-related fee' is very likely to fail under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, which treats charges for metered/ongoing water service as property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, so the court will reach and likely rule against the district on the cost-of-service merits rather than dismiss on a threshold ground.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify holding re: cost-based tiered charges)
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) — verify citation and holding re: tiered/drought-surcharge cost justification
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (procedural/validation-action implications, not merits)
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e) (Prop 26 tax definition and fee exceptions)
data.biggest_uncertainty: What dollar portion of the $0.85/hcf surcharge is actually attributable to the 1.6x cost differential versus pure conservation-incentive markup is not specified in the hypothetical, and this factual gap is likely dispositive of whether a court finds full compliance, partial invalidation, or total invalidation — without expert/rate-study evidence on this allocation, any probability estimate is necessarily a judgment call about how courts tend to resolve ambiguous cost-allocation records against the agency bearing the burden of proof.
